item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
only nitpick: why is the terminal asking for foundation creds, if it's a DoA computer?

Well it is called the SCP Department of Abnormalities for a reason, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the vorehole is gone, but something else just as terrifying is in its place.

so glad to see another DoA article, and another weird extradimensional city here.

only nitpick: why is the terminal asking for foundation creds, if it's a DoA computer?

anyway still a hard +1 for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I like this SCP, Pruwyben has voiced my own concerns regarding some of the structure of the article. That and the previous article was really good, including exploration logs etc, and this doesn't feel like it reaches that same level of writing. I know it's not fair to compare this article to the original story, it's just kind of hard, not to do so right now. I might need to come back and give this a reread later, for now I'll leave it as a no vote.

Does anyone happen to know why the original SCP-2678 was taken down? Like did the author give a reason, other than wanting it taken down?

item.title: A few questions/critiques
item.content:encoded:
The outpost is located on the outskirts of a 324.6 km² city in Italian Gothic architecture style, comprised exclusively of cathedrals, palaces and churches floating in a red-orange void.

This part confused me. It's phrased as if the buildings are floating in a void - so how do people walk around? Is the outpost in the void too?

No matter how long the organ is played, the buildings will remain stained.

"No matter how long" doesn't sound very scientific. I think it would help to indicate either a length of testing, or just say the rain has no effect on the bloodstains.

Symptoms do not appear to fade with time.

"Symptoms" would imply an illness, which isn't mentioned in the text. I would stick with "behavioral changes".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the DoA is just doing the job that the modern Foundation would do anyways? Having some terminals and lab equipment in the room with kid skeletons doesn't imply that they're containing or studying the thing any more than it implies that they are the organ's creators, especially considering the DoA's other articles.

Why include them in the piece at all if the article would work just as fine without them? It's like the article is trying to focus on two things; the Church's history of the mistreatment of children, and then the DoA trying to utilize a loyalty-generating anomaly for its own ends. It's way less punchy to have the piece be about one group doing a pretty bad thing (mind controlling its employees) with another group's pretty bad thing (dead child piano). It doesn't elaborate on the former at all and focuses on the latter for the punchline. It's defocusing itself trying to say "DoA bad" inside of a "Catholic Church bad" piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I +1d, but I do have a question.

SCP Dept of Abnormalities. In other DoA articles, it seems like the DoA preceeds the Foundation. Is this from a time of transition? And why is the plaque up if it's now an SCP site? Was it re-discovered, and if so, why not tell us when/how? If the terminal is hooked into SCiPnet, it seems like there would've been records of this site and a clear line of history, yet the article is written like it's been rediscovered by the foundation who otherwise didn't know it existed.

Just curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The DoA was containing/studying the object, just like the Foundation is now. They're no more complicit than the lock on the door.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this attempt better than the last, but the Department of Abnormalities part is still completely unrelated and tacked-on. If the metaphor is about the Catholic Church, why butcher it by making the Department of Abnormalities culpable for it instead? Catholicism is already name-dropped in the article, so having the perpetrator be the DoA instead1 shifts the in-universe blame off of the Catholic Church and puts it on to outside forces.

Let me put it another way; your metaphor is about the Catholic Church's past actions, but the Catholic Church is in that metaphor and, in it, is not the one responsible for those actions. The subtext has been mangled.

-1, because while I liked the rest of the piece quite a bit, the above issues undid anything good that this article had going for it.

Footnotes
1. Even if you assume the Church backed the project, it says DoA on the door and contains Foundation equipment. That implies that the DoA are at least the primary operative in this project.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, what happened to the vorehole? That was an old favorite of mine.
As someone who actively participates in a school choir, this one strikes a particular chord. Pun aside though, this is extremely well done in a strangely disturbing way. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is much, much better than the previous iteration. I'm impressed.
Edit: Just realized what the missing bones mean… :( +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The longest recorded testing of a key lasted 23 minutes, at which point the C7 key splintered beyond functionality. The remaining fragments were noted as bleeding for several days.

I'm pretty much upvoting for this alone. Here are a few examples of a 7th octave C note, for reference. They forced a child to scream that for 23 minutes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is good, but I am still sad that the Vorehole is gone.

I guess this slot had to be taken eventually. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite incredible how you manage to remaster these SCPs with such delicate touches, never removing the original message but tweaking minor aspects to achieve a better overall product. Obvious plus one from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I had a dream two nights ago. I saw a city all of blood, with towers made of bone, and blood ran in the streets like water."

A story about a shadowy organization and something horrible they found buried in the basement of the divine.

Thank you to ARD, Cybersqyd, Oboebandgeek99, Yossipossi, Lt flops, RockteethMothEyes, JackalRelated and TheMightyMcB for critting.

Some optional reading
- 

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Castrato

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catholic_Church_sexual_abuse_cases
